comments.text: I have written up the cases 7 ≤ n ≤ 13 of this problem: for each such n, a set of n points in the plane has at least two distances that each occur at least once but at most n times. The cases n = 7, 8, 9, 10, 13 are unconditional (n = 8 and n = 10 via descent-and-extension arguments verified in exact arithmetic); n = 11 and n = 12 rest on Wei's classification of 11-point 5-distance sets [Ars Combin. 102 (2011), 505–515], whose triangular-lattice part I re-derive independently. This is a finite result on small n and does not resolve the problem for general n. Note and verification code: https://github.com/JuanMarchetto/erdos-132-note . The formal statement of the problem is also contributed to the Formal Conjectures project (google-deepmind/formal-conjectures).

Hi all — I wanted to share a short update related to Erdős problem 132 (the “two distances with multiplicity in $[1,n]$” question). In a brief note I verify the conjectured Erdős–Pach statement for $n=7$, and I also give a structural reduction for $n=8$. For $n=7$: Let $A$ be a $7$-point set in the plane and let $m_A(d)$ be the multiplicity of distance $d$. If $|D(A)| \ge 4$, then it is impossible that at most one distance has multiplicity $\le 7$: indeed, if all but possibly one distance satisfy $m_A(d) \ge 8$, then $21 = \sum_{d \in D(A)} m_A(d) \ge 1 + 3\cdot 8 = 25$, a contradiction. Hence in this case there are at least two distances with multiplicity $\le 7$. So we may assume $|D(A)| \le 3$. Also $|D(A)| \ne 2$ since planar $2$-distance sets have size at most $5$. Therefore $|D(A)| = 3$ and $A$ is a $7$-point $3$-distance set. Using the known classification of maximum planar $3$-distance sets of size $7$ (regular heptagon $R_7$ or the “regular hexagon + center” configuration $R_6^+$), one checks directly that in each configuration at least two distances have multiplicity $\le 7$. This completes the verification for $n=7$. For $n=8$: A simple counting reduction shows that any counterexample must be extremely rigid. If $|D(A)| \ge 5$ and at most one distance has multiplicity $\le 8$, then $28 = \sum_{d \in D(A)} m_A(d) \ge 1 + 4\cdot 9 = 37$, contradiction, so any counterexample must satisfy $|D(A)| \le 4$. On the other hand $|D(A)|$ cannot be $3$ because $g(3)=7$ (no planar $3$-distance set has $8$ points). Hence a counterexample would have exactly $|D(A)| = 4$. Combining this with the diameter bound (there exists a distance with multiplicity $\le 8$; in particular the diameter has multiplicity $\le 8$), the only possible multiplicity profile is $\{1,9,9,9\}$, where the distance of multiplicity $1$ is the unique diameter and the other three distances occur exactly $9$ times each. In other words: an $n=8$ counterexample would have a unique diametral pair and the remaining $27$ pairs split into three distance classes of size $9$ each. The note (with references and a small numerical appendix) is available here: https://doi.org/10.13140/RG.2.2.26754.95684 Any feedback or ideas for ruling out the $n=8$ profile $\{1,9,9,9\}$ would be very welcome — it seems like a promising “bottleneck” structure for a full solution.

Looks like GPT-5.2 can do n=8 in a pretty straightforward way: Let $A\subset\mathbb R^2$ be a set of $8$ points. For $d>0$ define the multiplicity\[ m_A(d):=\bigl|\{\{x,y\}\subset A:\ |x-y|=d\}\bigr| \]and let $D(A)$ be the set of distances determined by $A$. We claim that there exist two distinct distances $d_1\neq d_2$ with $1\le m_A(d_i)\le 8$ for $i=1,2$. Assume for contradiction that at most one distance has multiplicity $\le 8$. Since $\sum_{d\in D(A)} m_A(d)=\binom82=28$, if $|D(A)|\ge 5$ then\[ 28=\sum_{d\in D(A)} m_A(d)\ge 1+4\cdot 9=37, \]a contradiction. Hence a counterexample must satisfy $|D(A)|\le 4$. It is known that there is no planar $3$-distance set of size $8$ (equivalently, $g_2(3)=7$), so $|D(A)|\neq 3$. Therefore any counterexample must have $|D(A)|=4$. Let $\Delta$ be the diameter of $A$. A classical diameter-graph lemma (Hopf--Pannwitz) implies that in an $n$-point planar set the diameter occurs at most $n$ times; hence here $m_A(\Delta)\le 8$. Under our counterexample assumption, $\Delta$ must be the unique distance with multiplicity $\le 8$, so the remaining three distances have multiplicity $\ge 9$. Since the total sum is $28$, this forces the multiplicity profile\[ \{m_A(d): d\in D(A)\}=\{1,9,9,9\}, \]in particular $m_A(\Delta)=1$ and $A$ has a unique diametral pair. Now invoke the classification of planar $8$-point $4$-distance sets: up to similarity, every such set is one of the following types: (1) the regular octagon $R_8$; (2) the regular heptagon together with its center $R_7^+$; (3) the exceptional configuration shown in the classification (often drawn as a square with four equilateral ''bumps''); (4) an $8$-point subset of one of the classified $9$-point $4$-distance sets. For each type one checks the distance multiplicities and finds at least two distinct distances of multiplicity $\le 8$: for $R_8$ the four distance classes have multiplicities $(8,8,8,4)$; for $R_7^+$ all four distances have multiplicity $7$; for the exceptional configuration two of the distances occur only twice; and in the remaining finitely many $8$-subsets arising from the $9$-point parents, the multiplicity tables show that no configuration has profile $\{1,9,9,9\}$. This contradicts the forced profile above. Hence no counterexample exists, and every $8$-point planar set determines at least two distances that occur in at most $8$ pairs.
